title: Tea on Carpet
timeEstimate: 15–40 minutes; alkaline cleaners are the classic mistake
stainChemistry: Tea is tea browning pigments + catechins — oxidized polyphenols (tannins) responsible for the amber-brown color. Tannins bond to carpet fibers through grip and hydrophobic interactions. Two things make tea harder than it looks: (1) tannins DARKEN when they contact alkaline products (ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most all-purpose sprays) — a pH above 7 shifts the color equilibrium toward the darker form, turning a faint tea ring into a deep brown stain that's often permanent, and (2) oxidation over time makes tannins lock together deeper into the fiber. Black tea is more pigmented than green tea — more polyphenol, more work. Heat (even hot tea still hot when it lands) lock tannin in faster. The #1 internet advice mistake on tea stains is reaching for baking soda or OxiClean.
whyThisWorks: Cold water + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide is the correct tannin chemistry: cold keeps the tannin mobile, Dawn lifts fat (if the tea had milk) and emulsifies, peroxide OXIDIZES the tea pigment into colorless compounds. White vinegar rinse at the end keeps the acid side (lighter color) of the equilibrium. The dry-down step prevents the pad from wicking residual tea back up as a ring.
defaultAssumption: Morning tea on the living room rug, iced tea spill, kid's tea party. Fresh + plain (black or green tea, no milk) = quick win with Dawn + peroxide. Older / bigger / with milk / artificial-flavor "tea" drinks = more rounds, but same protocol. If anyone already hit it with baking soda or OxiClean before you arrived, the color may be locked in.

spottingProtocol.technique: Absorb liquid before it reaches the pad
spottingProtocol.equipment: White paper towels or cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press a stack of white paper towels or a clean cloth STRAIGHT DOWN on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in. Don't rub — rubbing drives tea deeper into the pile and pad.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush fresh tea with small amount of cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pour a SMALL amount of cold water on the spot (don't flood — over-wetting sends tea into the pad). Blot immediately with a dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 times. Cold only — hot water lock tannin in deeper, even if the tea itself was hot.

spottingProtocol.technique: Verify peroxide doesn't lift carpet dye
spottingProtocol.instruction: In a hidden area (closet, under furniture), dab the Dawn + peroxide solution (see step 4) on a white cloth. Press on the hidden carpet 2 min. If carpet color transfers to the cloth, skip peroxide — it will bleach the carpet. Use Dawn alone instead.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + 3% peroxide in cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix 1 tbsp Dawn + 1 cup 3% H₂O₂ in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring damp (not dripping), press on the spot. Dwell 5–10 min. The peroxide oxidizes the tea browning pigments. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Fresh tea: 1–2 cycles. Black tea or older stain: 3 cycles.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia, bleach, or alkaline cleaners.

spottingProtocol.technique: Neutralize peroxide + keep color on acid side
spottingProtocol.equipment: 1 tbsp white vinegar + 1 cup cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute vinegar on a damp cloth. Press on the spot 5 min. Neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps tannin in its lighter (acid-side) form as it dries. Blot dry.
Stack dry white paper towels on the spot, weigh them with a heavy book, leave overnight. Pulls residual tea up and out of the pad before it can wick back as a visible ring next morning.
Inspect in natural light — tea rings that look gone when wet reappear as a pale tan halo when dry. Halo remaining = repeat steps 4–6 once more. Two home rounds is the ceiling.

steps: 2. Cold water dilute (small volume, blot between).
4. Dawn + 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth, 5–10 min, 1–3 cycles.
6. Weighted paper towels overnight — prevents wick-back.
7. Daylight inspect; one more round if needed.
warnings: NEVER alkaline cleaners on tea — ammonia / baking soda / OxiClean / most all-purpose sprays PERMANENTLY DARKEN tannin
NEVER hot water — lock tannin in deeper
Hidden-area test peroxide on dyed carpet
Never pour water — over-wetting sends tea into the pad

homeSolutions.instruction: Press a thick stack of white paper towels or a clean white cloth STRAIGHT DOWN on the tea. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the OUTSIDE of the spill inward. Don't rub — rubbing pushes tea deeper into the pile and into the pad underneath, and pad contamination is where the ring comes back days later.

homeSolutions.agent: The Tannin Rule — No Alkaline Cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: This is the hard rule for tea and every tannin stain (wine, coffee, berries). ALKALINE products (pH above 7) turn tannin brown and lock it in. That includes: baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean, Mr. Clean, Simple Green, most all-purpose sprays. If someone already hit this spot with one of those, the color may be locked. Do NOT reach for any of them now. Your toolkit here is: cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% hydrogen peroxide (drugstore brown bottle), and white vinegar. Nothing else.

Pour a SMALL amount of cold water on the spot — about as much as was spilled. Immediately blot with a dry cloth. Repeat 2–3 times. Fresh tea is water-soluble; early dilution pulls most of the color out. Cold only — even if the tea was hot, use cold now. Hot water lock together the tannin deeper. DO NOT flood the spot with water — if it reaches the pad, it carries tea down there where you can't extract it.

In a closet or under furniture, dab a tiny bit of the Dawn + peroxide mix (from step 5) on a white cloth, press on the carpet 2 minutes. If the cloth picks up carpet color, skip peroxide entirely — use Dawn + cold water alone. If the test is clean, proceed.

Mix 1 tablespoon Dawn dish soap with 1 cup of 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide. Dampen a white cloth in the mix, wring it out so it's damp not dripping, press on the spot. Let it sit 5–10 minutes. The peroxide oxidizes the brown tannin pigment into colorless compounds; the Dawn lifts. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Fresh tea clears in 1–2 cycles; black tea or older staining may need 3.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with bleach, ammonia, OxiClean, or any alkaline cleaner. Do not layer chemistries.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 tablespoon white vinegar with 1 cup cool water. Dab on a damp cloth, press on the spot 5 minutes. Neutralizes any peroxide residue and keeps any remaining tannin in its lighter (acidic) color rather than drifting back toward brown. Blot dry. Don't skip this step — it's what separates "looks fine wet, ring comes back dry" from actually clean.

Stack 6–10 sheets of white paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, leave overnight. Pulls residual tea out of the pad before it wicks back as a visible ring. Don't walk on the spot or vacuum until fully dry.

homeSolutions.agent: Daylight Inspect, One More Round If Needed
homeSolutions.instruction: Inspect in daylight the next morning. Tea ghosts often look invisible when wet and reappear as a pale tan halo when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 once more. Two rounds is the home ceiling.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — Pro for Wool, Alkaline-Set, or Ring Return
homeSolutions.instruction: Call a pro if: the carpet is wool (pH-sensitive, and peroxide is riskier), someone already hit the spot with baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia (tannin may be locked), the ring keeps coming back, or two full rounds haven't cleared it. Tell them: "Tea on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + white vinegar rinse. No alkaline products used [or: baking soda/OxiClean was used before I started]." That disclosure matters — it tells them what chemistry is still on the table.

steps: 2. NO alkaline cleaners — no baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia.
3. Small-volume cold water dilute, blot between.
6. White vinegar + cool water rinse — DON'T skip.
8. Daylight inspect; one more round max.
9. Pro for wool, alkaline-set, ring return.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia was already used (tannin may be locked), if the carpet is wool and peroxide lifts color on a hidden test, or if the ring keeps returning after two rounds.
warnings: NEVER alkaline cleaners — turns tea brown permanently
NEVER hot water — lock tannin in deeper
Never rub — drives tea into the pad
Never flood with water — pad wicks tea back as a ring
Never mix peroxide with ammonia / bleach / alkaline

neverDo: Never baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia — locks tannin
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester carpet: tolerates Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar
Wool carpet: pH-sensitive — hidden test mandatory, peroxide risky
Dyed carpet: hidden-area peroxide test mandatory

materialWarnings: Alkaline cleaners are the classic tannin mistake
Cold water only — even on hot-tea spills
Vinegar rinse prevents the ring from coming back

escalation.when: Alkaline cleaner already used on the spot, wool carpet, ring keeps returning after two home rounds, or carpet lifts color on peroxide hidden-test.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tea on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + white vinegar. [No alkaline used / alkaline was used before I got here]. [Ring returns / color locked in brown / hidden-test failed]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with tannin formula

contextQuestions: Black tea, green tea, herbal, or a flavored bottled tea?
Has anyone hit it with baking soda / OxiClean / ammonia?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Milk in the tea adds a small protein+fat component — Dawn handles it before peroxide. Bottled tea drinks with added artificial dye may need more rounds. The alkaline-locking phenomenon is the biggest bad-advice magnet — Google results commonly recommend baking soda and that's exactly wrong.
